oai_dc:dc.dc:title: Low-stress people handling – What our livestock can teach us about communication
oai_dc:dc.dc:creator: Nicholls, Ashley
oai_dc:dc.dc:subject: stockmanship
employee retention
leadership
communication
oai_dc:dc.dc:description: Low-stress cattle handling has become a global standard be­cause it improves safety, animal welfare and performance by using pressure and release, flight zones and thoughtful facil­ity design. These same principles can be adapted to manag­ing people in cattle operations. This paper outlines a practical framework for low-stress people handling that mirrors low-stress stockmanship: leaders learn individual flight zones, re­duce fear of novelty through clarity, and troubleshoot stalls in performance by identifying root causes rather than escalating pressure. Emphasis is placed on breaking high-pressure habits, training for understanding rather than compliance, and ensur­ing staff can see a path forward through development. The goal is to support retention, reduce incidents and improve day-to-day execution by creating workplaces where people feel safe, capable and connected to the operation’s mission.
oai_dc:dc.dc:source: American Association of Bovine Practitioners Conference Proceedings; No. 1 (2026): 2026 Recent Graduate Conference Proceedings; 5-6
oai_dc:dc.dc:rights: Copyright (c) 2026 American Association of Bovine Practitioners
